speaker: interviewer
text: It's June 7, 1995. We are interviewing Cantor Morris Lang. This is reel four. Cantor, now-- now we have to get into the [? takless ?]. You arrived at Calgary. And you are interviewed for a proba. And the one who interviewed you was the rabbi. It wasn't the committee. It was just the rabbi. Tell me about the proba.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was in New York at the United Synagogue Shul. And they took me in a room. And the rabbi had already interviewed about 12, 11 or 12 candidates. And I was the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to me, are you Morris Lang, [INAUDIBLE] Morris Lang? Tell me, what kind of a hazzan are you? So I figured to myself, at that time my wife had already a little [? stow ?]. And I didn't go with intentions to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To get the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to get the job. And I learned look at that time that if you're not too eager something in life, you'll get it. Because if you're too eager, you may not get it. So he said to me, what kind of hazzan are you? So I lift up my hands. And I said, I'm not a hazzan. I'm a hazzan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, yeah? Could you demonstrate something for me? And you in the Yom Kippur liturgy, we have a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means the hazzan voice-- lifts his voice to God. And he says, God, dear God, you are the one that you know all the hidden things in a person, and everything. So I thought to myself. And I started to say, [SINGING IN HEBREW] He said, stop. So I stopped. He said, tell me. Are you [? a balcora ?] too? Could you read from the Torah? I said, I think so. I think in my own way I think I'm a good [? balcora. ?] He said, how would you read? What do we read on the first day of Rosh Hashanah? So I told him from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He said, would you demonstrate for me a piece? So he gave me the [? homation ?]. I said, I'll tell you what, Rabbi, you watch me in [? homation ?], for I don't make mistakes. And of course, over every Shabbas I read-- I read a different way.

speaker: interviewee
text: But for Rosh Hashanah, I have a different way of reading. [INAUDIBLE] for Rosh Hashanah. And I said, [SINGING IN HEBREW] Whatever it went through his mind, I don't remember. But I know that when I left, I had another interview, which is a story in itself. And I didn't want to accept it. And when I came home, three days later, I got a call from the rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, Cantor Lang, tell me, have you taken another position yet? I said, no. But I am-- I am-- I'm thinking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'm thinking because I have two other places I have to make up my mind with to go. He said, if you promise me that you don't go, you don't have to be interviewed anymore, you have a job with us for Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. And he got in with me in details how much and everything. And he said to me, and we'll pay you-- your--

speaker: interviewer
text: Transportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: --transportation. And you make your own arrangement. We'll pay you for it. And I said, but I want to come with my wife. He said, well, my all means. We'll-- we'll provide for it. But you have to pay the transportation for my-- for your wife yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Rabbi, I'll tell you something. No way. I am not accepting your position because this is an insult to me. And I said, Rabbi, would you want to be high holidays without your wife? And maybe I can afford it. I can't afford to pay [INAUDIBLE]. But this is not [NON-ENGLISH], I said. He said, wait. Hold a minute. He said, there's a board meeting. Hold the line. Hold the line. And he came back. And he said--

speaker: interviewer
text: You got it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you got it. Just make your own arrangement. When I came back to this town, they didn't know what to do for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was just for Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: But once they heard you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was-- their mind, was maybe if I'm good for [? Rosh Hashanah and ?] Yom Kippur, maybe I could be accepted. You know, in my mind, wasn't that I will accept the [? Shtalar ?] so far away from the-- from the-- from my children, my family. So we came there. And there was the president of the congregation. And there was the vice president, and their president from the ritual committee. There were three--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Halibutitati. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? --Halibatibati. ?] And they came. And they took me. And I said, first of all, what happened to the cantor before? And he said, unfortunately, six weeks before Rosh Hashanah, or before me, he passed away. And I told him who it was. So I said, before I go Sunday, I want to go in to express my-- express condolences to his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they thought that that was nice. They took me. And I came. I said, I'm sorry what happened to your husband. Like to say it's not my fault that I came here as the cantor. And then they set us up in a-- in a motel, which was in walking distance from the shul. And I had a rehearsal with the choir, which we all-- they send me the music and I-- you know, by telephone. And I had one rehearsal.

speaker: interviewer
text: One rehearsal?

speaker: interviewee
text: One rehearsal with the choir. And I arranged everything on one Tuesday night. I remember Wednesday night was [? Rashuna tadaven ?]. Because it was Thursday, Friday, Shabbas. And I got-- and then I didn't know that there was a committee of people sitting behind the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The curtains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Curtains. And they invited me out for-- for a cup of coffee after this. And they said to me right away, you know, we heard you arranging. We liked what you did. Well, it made me feel good. And then I said to them, if you have any desire to invite me out somewhere here, please, make sure that I have kosher food. And they said, by all means, we will arrange that with you. But I [? dealt ?] with Rosh Hashanah there, the choir. And it came out very good. Then on Yom Kippur was this-- it broke out the Six-Day War. And you know, Calgary, in Israel is eight hours difference. And, you know, we-- we heard right away what happened and everything. And I remember that-- that in that time I got up to chant the [? kimerla rachan ?] for the 6 million [? kadoshian ?] and also for the fallen soldiers in Israel. It-- I tore my heart out. And that they told me that it was so that everyone cried.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got too [INAUDIBLE] to negotiate to come and live there. And I said, Pearl-- what do you think? She said, we're gonna move to Calgary? What do you mean? Leave the children and the grandchildren? We had at that time two grandchildren. Leave the grandchildren [? and then ?] [? there? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: But slowly, slowly they offered me a nice position. And we lived there for 16 years. We were very happy there. And of course, you know, you could be as far away from family as you want. My wife traveled a lot of times to Washington to visit them. And they, the children came for summer because Calgary has cold weather. But summers are very nice there. It's only two months summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Two months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months. And sometimes we could get snow in the middle of summer there too. But-- and we kept in touch with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have three children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me something about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My first daughter was born in [? Amburg, ?] [? Oberfell, ?] in Germany. When this child was born, this was to us everything. It was the crown of our heads. Then five years later, we lived at that time already-- we lived in-- we were in Brooklyn already. Our first son was born, Abe, or Abraham Isaac. And, you know, when my daughter was born, we will rename him. My mother's name was Baila Sarah. And my wife's names-- my wife's mother's name, my mother-in-law's name was Leah. So we decided that Sara Leah would be a nice-- so she's called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Sara Leah. And we are very proud of her. And she still is our crown. Now, our son was born. I got meshuggah. To get a son and a Lang that was-- he was born I think in-- in-- in Brooklyn. And I remember at the [? bris, ?] when we had the bris, I-- I didn't know how many flowers to bring to my wife. And of course, we moved at that time to Williamsport. And my young-- and our youngest son, Phillip Kalman of [? Hyval ?] Kalman was born in Williamsport, Pennsylvania. And since then, I moved on. And I moved to Washington. So all the children came. And of course, Sara was married. And then the other two sons were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave you seven--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave me-- they gave us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seven?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't say me. It's Pearl and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're absolutely right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pearl and me. I mean-- and they gave us seven--

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: --beautiful grandchildren. The first four grandsons-- I thought I'll never see a granddaughter. And of course, the first one was my son's, my oldest son's daughter. Jennifer was born. And later down, [? Miriam, ?] was born to my youngest son. And Lenor-- we call [? her Nani ?]. So we have seven beautiful grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. The last-- last question I think is the important question of why we've come to this interview. And you have some thoughts about those people who deny the Holocaust and the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] that you want to express to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'd like to hear about that now.

speaker: interviewee
text: For what we've went though in life, and what we're still going through. I mean, to sit down and give a-- a testimony like this is not easy for us. And when I hear that there are people in this world who come out with books and deny the Holocaust, great anti-semites, because they say that it is propaganda, that we just made up this, it hurts my soul. It hurts myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because how in the world? Because I think that the tragedy is so great. The [? Shua ?], the Holocaust, is so tremendous great that-- that even people come to deny it. And this hurts me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that in this day and age, we should be very watchful. We should keep an eye. After all, we live among non-Jews. And I know that anti-semitism hasn't diminished. All the time it gets greater.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think we should watch out, that, God forbid, history of this sort should not repeat itself. And-- and for this, that my wife and I have lived through so many concentration camps. And we never lost our faith in God. And maybe this was the only one thing that helped us to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where my brothers and sisters did not survive, and my father and mother, and-- and my old relatives, therefore, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And I chant [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And I thank God for sparing our life, and for taking care of us as we say daily, and every time, morning, evening, even midday, and for all the goodness, that I chant. I chanted with [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], with heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: So therefore, I'd like to finish up with this chant, which you asked me, I should give you a chant. And I sing the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. [SINGING IN HEBREW] Cantor Lang, it's been a pleasure speaking with you and spending time with you. And I wish you many years of [? nalas ?] with your children, and happiness, and good health with your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. This is Pearl, my wife. We going to be celebrating on September the 1st, 50 year-- 50 years of marriage. And I think I'm-- I have always been happy with you, Pearl [INAUDIBLE]. And believe me, in every day, I love you more and more. And God should help us. We should both be well. Especially you should be healthy and have lots of [? nalas ?] from our children and grandchildren. And I can tell you, Pearl, you have been always helpful in-- in all my undertakings in lifetime. And I have once, I think, expressed when we left Calgary. And I'll say the same thing to you. It should be on record, that if they would give me the whole world of diamonds, I wouldn't take for this one Pearl. And I give you a kiss. This is a picture of my bubbe Braindel. This is so precious to me because this is the only grandmother that I have known while I was in Europe. And when I came here, I found a little picture by my uncle of his mother, because he and my mother were sister and brothers. So I'm very proud. This is Bubbe Braindel. This is my sister, my elder sister, Golda. She was married. And she fought with the SS men. She didn't want to give up her only infant baby. I'm proud to have this picture with me. And more I look at her, more I remember her with great love. This in my elder brother, Sholom. Sholom was with me in all the camps. We survived all the camps. And then two years after the war he had some struggle with German police. And they shot him to death on May 25, 1947. This is a picture of my lovely wife and myself. This picture was taken to our-- at our 40th wedding anniversary about 10 years ago. This is-- Those are my two sons, a picture of Abe and Philip. And it was taken about eight years ago. This is a picture of my dear wife and my dear daughter, Sara. I love this picture. I love them both very much. This is a picture of our entire family, of which we are very proud. And it was taken just about a month ago, over a month ago, at our first granddaughter's bat mitzvah. And to describe this picture, how the family is situated in here on this picture, it is from left to right. The first one is [? Kell ?], our-- our-- our daughter-in-law. She is the wife of Abe. Next to her is our son, Abe. Next to Abe is our grandson, [? Enby ?]. And then after [? Enby ?] is Philip. And next to him is his wife, Monique. And then I come in with my little head in the middle there. And I go on the top line there is Mikey, our grandson. Next to Mikey stands Leo, grandson. Next to Leo is his father, Norman. And next to Norman is our first grandson, Ronnie [? the lawyer. ?] And the first line are the women. My wife, from left to right-- then of course comes again-- no. First [? is Kell, ?] my wife, Monique, [? Miriam, ?] our granddaughters, and Jennifer, [? Leanor, ?] and of course, my daughter-- our daughter. I shouldn't say my daughter-- our daughter, Sara. God bless them all. And we are very happy to have our family. We started with two. And now [? Canahora ?], I'm looking at 15 people. Of course, this is myself, the picture that I used to wear in my gown and [? kippur ?] when I was conducting service. That means when I was working. This picture was taken about six, seven years ago. Now I'm a retired hazzan. And I live in Florida very happily with my wife.
